attributes.stem:
A 35-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic for review following a workplace injury two weeks ago. He sustained a fractured ankle while working as a metalworker. He has no other significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

He has been cleared by an orthopaedic surgeon to weight bear as tolerated with a walking boot for the next four weeks. He reports no pain or discomfort at present. His work duties typically involve prolonged standing, heavy lifting, and working in confined spaces. Both he and his employer are keen for him to return to work.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate return to work plan?
options: Modified office-based duties with gradual increase in physical tasks
Remain off work until the walking boot is removed
Modified duties with immediate return to heavy lifting
Return to normal duties but with reduced hours
attributes.explanation:
This case highlights the importance of appropriate return to work planning following workplace injuries. The key considerations are the nature of the injury, current functional capacity, and work requirements. Given the importance of returning to work from a psychological, social and economic point of view, many people are keen too return to work as soon as possible, but this should be well planned to ensure an optimal recovery and rehabilitation.

Modified office-based duties with gradual increase in physical tasks represents the most appropriate approach. This allows the worker to maintain workplace engagement while protecting the healing ankle. Tasks can be progressively upgraded as function improves, following a structured rehabilitation plan.

Return to full duties immediately would place excessive stress on the healing fracture and risk re-injury. The patient's normal duties involve prolonged standing and heavy lifting, which are not compatible with the current phase of recovery.

Remain off work until the walking boot is removed is unnecessarily restrictive. Many workers can safely return to modified duties while recovering, which has benefits for both psychological well-being and maintaining work relationships.

Return to normal duties but with reduced hours does not adequately address the risk factors in this case. Even with reduced hours, activities like prolonged standing and heavy lifting could compromise healing, delay recovery or cause re-injury.

Modified duties with immediate return to heavy lifting would be inappropriate as heavy lifting could place excessive stress on the healing ankle, particularly given the need for stable positioning and balance during lifting tasks.

The key principles of return to work planning include clear communication between all parties, graduated increase in activities, regular review of progress, and modification of duties to avoid aggravating factors while maintaining meaningful work engagement.
learningPoints: A graduated return to work plan should be implemented following workplace injuries to prevent re-injury and ensure sustainable recovery.
Modified duties should be identified that avoid aggravating factors while maintaining workplace engagement.
Clear communication between healthcare providers, worker, and employer is essential for successful return to work outcomes.
references: The Royal Australasian College of Physicians, Australasian Faculty of Occupational and Environmental Medicine Policy on preventing work disability, Sydney 2010. Available online at ; https://www.racp.edu.au/docs/default-source/policy-and-adv/afoem/hbgw/helping-people-return-to-work-using-evidence-for-better-outcomes.pdf?sfvrsn=57ae3e1a_10
Fenner P. Returning to work after an injury. Aust Fam Physician. 2013 Apr;42(4):182-5. PMID: 23550239. Available online at ; https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2013/april/returning-to-work/
